speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Nina.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is August 6, 1995. The survivor we're interviewing is Abe Danko. He was born Abe Dankowitz. The interviewer is Nina Dorf. We are in Morton Grove, Illinois, the United States of America. And the language for the interview is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Nina Dorf. This is August 6, 1995. And today we're interviewing Abe Danko, born Dankowitz. We're in Morton Grove, Illinois, United States of America. And we're going to be doing the interview in English. OK, Mr. Danko, the first thing we're going to do is I'm going to ask you to tell us your name and spell it. My name is Abe Danko.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. A-B-E D-A-N-K-O. And you were born with a different name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Dankowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-A-N-K-O-W-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czestochowa, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Can you spell Czestochwoa for us? C-Z-E-N-S-T-O-C-H-O-W-A. Very good. When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in May 24, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm 75.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. Now we're going to spend this first tape talking about your growing-up years and your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to start by telling us about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Europe?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have-- when I was born, my mother died. I was 11 weeks old, and my mother passed away. And my father didn't plan to remarry. So they try to-- I have a older brother, three years older for me. So they tried to give away to the grandparents. But meanwhile, the-- one grandparents passed away [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a son-in-law. And they got two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took it already, the two children. They said they couldn't take a third one. So they give me away for-- to raise me. But it didn't help I got always sick. So after two years, my father remarried. And they took me home. And I don't remember. That's what I was told the story. All right. Well, let's just go back for a minute. Because I want to get it clear. After your mother died, they gave you away to be raised to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know to whom.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, to somebody in town.

speaker: interviewee
text: For money, for money, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, like a foster mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, yeah. To sleep over there and eat over there. Yeah, and they paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see your dad during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I was too small to say-- tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where was your older brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was with my grandparents, with the father's parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your older brother was with your father's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Did already have two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: --had the cousins.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with a foster mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but then something happened when you were two years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because I was-- I have a big belly. And I couldn't walk. And I got crooked legs, you know. They call-- they called it some sickness. By us they call it the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They call it the English Sickness?

speaker: interviewee
text: The English Sickness, right. So they took me-- my father remarried, and they took me home. And I didn't remember anything. But I remember when I was already five, and I started going to cheder. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So after then, I was seven. I started going to cheder and an-- and a public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're going to public school from 8:00 to 12:00. And from 1:00 to 7:00, you're going to cheder. And 8:30, you go to sleep. Because tomorrow by 8 o'clock, supposed to be in school. So I was [INAUDIBLE] the work. What do you remember about your stepmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Should tell you the true, I didn't have special privileges, or special-- she didn't do nothing special wrong to me. Because she has other two children right away. So you know, if she give something, she give $0.10 more to the stepbrother, which I don't know. Listen-- what you know you don't know. But otherwise, I didn't saw any different. And when I finished school, 14, they took me to machine-- start working.

speaker: interviewer
text: A sewing machine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your dad was also a tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad was a tailor, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you-- did-- you learned to work with your dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: With your dad, right. Because the older brother work already with him. And after then, they brought you in when you was already 13. I already helped it out, too. Because one time they needed work. So they skipped today cheder, skipped today the school, you know, and helping out today. Sew buttons, you know, clean up. You know, we got our, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your dad have his own shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: We made it and we selled it. You know, I don't know how to explain to you, it's-- you know, like here is on Maxwell Street, you know. You-- every-- every day you go out on the market and sell it. And in the next city is a special market day. So we took a-- hired a horse and buggy. And we packed everything. And we go over there. You know, but three of us-- four-- my stepmother did. Because we had always a, a, a helper in house to cook and to clean. Mother was only in the business. She only sells it in this way. My father only was a cutter and helping out sewing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was his own business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you made up clothes, and then took them to sell at markets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he worked, and your stepmother, and you two older boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a time, we worked with three boys. Because I got another stepbrother. He was already working too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the baby-- who took care of the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which baby?

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a fourth, wasn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was the girl--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the girl in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house, right. So that's all--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Polish girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Polish girl. She was 24 hours a day. She get 10 zlotys a month, with food. She was the cook. She was the cleaner. She was everything. And then on Christmas you gave her a present. This was the rules over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So having somebody live in the house-- did that make you fairly-- were you a well-to-do family? Or what would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you see we have a three-- three rooms. In Europe, is not like here. In Europe we was used to be just four beds in one room. And we was even not ashamed to sleep two people in one bed. But the girl, she got-- the parents slept in the last room. In the middle room, we have the-- like the [NON-ENGLISH], like the storage from the-- for the-- what we sew.

speaker: interviewer
text: The clothes and stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but we pushed in a bed. And the girl slept over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we four brothers slept in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where'd you sleep in the kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep in the kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a bed in the kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was four beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was four beds in the kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Was one bed was a table and a bed. You ever heard of it, a table in a bed?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The table was-- you-- like this one. After then, you pick up the deck and you choke it out this one. [INAUDIBLE]. We didn't have mattresses. We have straw and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a sack of straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So one sack of straw went on top of the table.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not on top of the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Underneath?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a box, you know. And inside was the straw-- a sack with straw. But when you through sleeping, you just push it together. And you put the deck on top. And you work on the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Sounds OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We pressed on the table-- everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you worked-- the clothing store was out of the house, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not a clothing store.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand, but the business.

speaker: interviewee
text: The business was on the market. The business was like-- it was like a store, but it's not a built store. It was like a barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So where were-- the sewing machines were also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sewing machine was in the halls.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the hall.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, we got four machines in the halls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so was this in an apartment building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Czestochowa was a big city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czestochowa was about 45,000 Jews and 130,000 or 135,000 population.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember about Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot of thing. It was the pogroms in Czestochowa, where they-- one-- a butcher [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] killed a Gentile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why. But there was a pogrom. And we couldn't go out and-- you see, the Jews mostly live like in a ghetto. You know, it was not a ghetto. But it was-- this section lived the Jews. So the Jews was-- we never was afraid of them. But if they come and they-- in big-- in big quantity, big [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jews didn't go out. So-- but there was strong Jews-- boxers and everything there was not afraid. So they helped out. They say let's go come into the market. Come to this one. They helped out to this one. But it calmed down. When was it? How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, maybe [INAUDIBLE] was 14 or 15. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The antisemitism was big, very big. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was-- you couldn't walk in every-- every street what you want to walk. Like you want to go in park, you cannot go in park. It was-- I don't know how they recognize a Jew right away. Even if you didn't talk none, they recognize you as a Jew. So I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember antisemitism, even before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very big.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kinds of antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, whatever you're doing was no good. Whatever you going to do is no good. You see, there was-- the-- Poland has a law that you're not supposed to kill the Jew. But if someone beats you up, it's OK. You see, if they're staying here, and they wanted-- you come in and you want to buy something-- a pair of pants, or this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he say, hey, don't buy by the Jew. Well of course, who come? The Gentiles, from this-- from the small towns, from the villages. So he say, hey don't buy by the Jew. You cannot say nothing. Hey, go away. He has a right to, to boycott your stuff. According to the government, he has a right, and do something. So always was-- So what we did, like, for example, we got that Gentile girl. We took it already out a license on her name. And we put her. And after then, was one, a shoemaker-- he was a Gentile. We build them up so he be a, a seller for this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: We only charge him what the material cost and the work. Any profit that makes after the work is his. And, you know, Europe was a difference, because the work was cheap, but the food was cheap. For example, if you buy a pair of pants for $2-- the whole pants-- and the material cost you a $1.50, so what cost you--

speaker: interviewer
text: How you can sell it for $2 if the material cost $1.50? And the lining-- and this will cost you $0.20. So $0.30 for the work. So you got $2. You sell it. You got $0.30. You make 10 pairs a day, you got $3 a day. And $3 a day was average what a factory man worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because used-- they didn't hire Jews to a factory. They didn't allow it. Even they-- a Jew-- a Jew-- a Jew what has the factory, he wouldn't hire a Jew to the factory. Because he was scared that they're going to put a fire on the factory or something. He never hired a Jew to the factory, even if it was a Jewish factory. So the Jewry must be self-- supporting themself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And $3 a day was enough?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a average what-- listen, $3 a day, you're not alone. Your wife helped out, if you're married. And you got children, like me-- we was four people working. So we got $12 a day. But it's the question, if you sell it so much, there's a reason you need the market.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the market was that we got the two [NON-ENGLISH] this one, the shoemaker and the girl. But after then, the girl was too big. Because she say, I don't want anymore work in the house. I want to be this, when she makes money over there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So somebody must take care on the house. Nobody can take care on the house. So-- and Jewish girls doesn't want to go. So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did we do? Nothing, what we do. Once-- once a week we cooked up. We left with the mother. You helped out. We did what we can.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who did the cooking then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother, the stepmother, in the night. What can you do? It was not-- it was not a easy life. It's not America. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember you were telling me yesterday about-- that they were very observant Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to talk about that a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, in Europe, all Jews was observed. There was not such a thing Conservative and this one. If you're a Jew, you're a Jew. You're Jewish because you're observing. And if you-- unless you're a communist. Because every Jew was an observer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you weren't a communist then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Or you-- are a communist, which he doesn't believe. Or you're a Jew. We was-- I just told you. We was praying. Every day we put on the [INAUDIBLE]. Even we make it faster, and father said, hey, your head is not swell up. You just put it down, already put it down? You measured the head, if it's not swelling up? You know. We try to do what we can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you daven in the morning? At home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, yeah. Oh, we didn't have time. Because you see, we didn't use light. To work, we only used daylight, the daylight. We wake up [INAUDIBLE] in the summer, at 6:00. And you work until 6:00, 7:30 in the night. And we didn't even go to-- for-- supper was in the middle of the day. There was not such as-- like here you go for supper out. Supper-- and she brought the machine. The girl brought the machine the food-- potatoes, with a borscht, with [NON-ENGLISH]-- hamburgers, you know. You eat. You finish. You start work again. Because the time was-- catch the light. Because we don't work-- in this time was I think you supposed to pay for 1 kilowatt four, four hours work, for one kilowatt. Was so high, the electricity, that we mostly didn't use electricity in, in [INAUDIBLE]. But we do the best what we can. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Jewish holidays? Do you remember the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which holidays do you Remember

speaker: interviewee
text: All the holidays. All holidays. As a matter of fact, in our house really was a small-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what do you call it? A small-- not a synagogue, a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: A shtiebel, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: A shtiebel. And they call it [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, when you're poor a girl, and if you cannot get married because of money, this, this one helped you out. And only Friday they can get-- you can get married for them. And they give you 25 zlotlys, you know. And they prepare, give you a supper on Friday night. And that's what you get. But never-- they say never a Jew-- a Jewish wedding will fall apart because of money. So this was their slogan. And we was going. There was a shul. I remember when I was 16 years old, I was singing by a Hazzan He was the biggest in Poland, in Czestochowa. He was taken away to Australia. I get 25 zloty and two tickets for the parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the high holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the high holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had tickets in Europe, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, you didn't know this?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the synagogue. See, it was only two synagogues in the city. The rest was all-- oh, you got-- in every house, almost, a, a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: A little--

speaker: interviewee
text: A little this one, yeah. But the synagogue-- there was the, the German synagogue. And there was the state synagogue. In the state synagogue, they used to give concert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you sang with the cantor?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the cantor-- our choir. It was a choir.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the choir?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the choir.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still have a good voice?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No good?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I lost it. I used to have a good voice.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to sing something for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's where we was going. But we never-- we didn't belong to the synagogue, because oh, it was very big-- big shots. We belong to [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], you know-- to the little ones. When my grandparents lived, we used to belong to [NON-ENGLISH]. Because the grandparents lived in the same house where [NON-ENGLISH] is. So this reason, I was going even cheder over there, in the [NON-ENGLISH] because the grandparents took care of me-- I mean, the second grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: The stepmother's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: The stepmother's parents, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like at your cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who remember cheder?

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: When you go-- start going to cheder, you think cheder, you think something else-- to play, this one. Who remembers cheder? I remember cheder when there used to be-- I don't know there was a law or not that said rabbi has a right to hit you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I was in-- after my grandparents passed away-- so I was going [INAUDIBLE]. So he has this one. And he hits you with a stick.

speaker: interviewer
text: A ruler or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: A ruler, right. So he-- one guy, he hit him so hard. And he starts-- this one. And he was so nervous. And he took the ink holder. And he throw at him. And he cut him out an eye. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was blind all the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi. What can you did to, to the boy? Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you get hit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, plenty times. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Plenty times. He smoked cigarettes. But he never got a match. So he said, take my cigarette. Go out and to the neighbor over and lighted up the cigarette. So we go out. And we light it up. We bring him and say, rabbi, take fast, because it's burning already. [LAUGHS] And we smoked it out there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You smoked the rabbi's cigarette?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you got--

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? Everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got hit for that, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: And how. Everybody got hit. Yeah, it was-- listen, we was no angels.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about holidays at home? Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was observant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any particular holiday at home? Do you remember Pesach, maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. Pesach. All holidays was very good observed. Because you see, we was not rich. But we could afford it. See, I don't remember when I was small. But when we working already, four people working-- you can say five people, when mother works too-- so we could afford it. We have food-- enough food. We have every Saturday an apple, you know, for dessert. And you take a nap. Even the rabbi-- he comes-- he comes already Saturday. He wants to have a free meal. So he say to the father, I am going to show you what I teach your son today, this week. And he takes out the [NON-ENGLISH]. And meanwhile, he wants to grab a free meal. And what--

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, kids are stupid. I was like soccer-- socking playing-- soccer. So Friday I told him, Sunday I'm going to be sick. Because I want to go to the game. But what a kid know? He goes ahead and tells my father that I told him Sunday going to be sick. And he ask me, why you going to be sick? Because I want to go to the game. You going to-- you're going to have a game. So you didn't get to go to the game?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Stupidity.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were a trouble-maker, when you were a kid, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a trouble maker. I was not-- never a trouble maker. Listen, I used to play soccer. When I was 17, I used to be in the piazza, in the colosseum, and you play soccer. And they charge already $0.50 to enter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't get nothing for it. But I play. I liked to play soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me-- we were talking about Pesach.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the Seder was like at your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like at here-- maybe more observant. Maybe more observant. And if you want to make a few extra pennies, you boil up a, a-- [INAUDIBLE] oil kettle, and put fire on the bottom. And you have dishes-- come on, $0.10 cents a dip. [LAUGHS] It was-- it was-- you know-- everything was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Matzohs-- you don't buy matzohs by the pound. You buy matzohs by-- there was a curb, you know. And you must bring in your own [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH] mean? No, I don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] mean where you're covering-- you're covering yourself a blanket. And on top of the blanket, what are you putting on? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You cover the blanket.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bedspread.

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- not a bedspread. You put the blanket in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I know what you mean. But I forget the word too.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, you put the blanket in. So you bring this one in. Because if there rain, you going to [INAUDIBLE] the matzoh. That's-- the matzoh's no-- no-- not kosher anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't want to get your matzohs wet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cannot be this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you buy your matzoh?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bakery. In the bakery. And you stay with the-- in the griddle. You know, they make the holes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't have to worry about this. But they're just telling me that we're at the end of this first tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, oh, oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're going to stop now.
